passages.explanation: Very early in the morning, the chief priests, with the elders, the teachers of the law, and the whole Sanhedrin, make their plans. They bind Jesus, lead him away, and hand him over to Pilate. Pilate asks: are you the king of the Jews? You have said so, Jesus replies. The chief priests accuse him of many things. So Pilate asks again: aren't you going to answer? See how many things they're accusing you of. But Jesus still makes no reply, and Pilate is amazed. The transfer from the Sanhedrin to Pilate shifts the charge from religious (blasphemy) to political (claiming kingship). Jesus' silence before Pilate fulfils Isaiah 53:7 — like a sheep before its shearers is silent. Pilate's amazement suggests he's never seen a prisoner refuse to defend himself.

Now it's the custom at the festival to release a prisoner whom the people request. A man called Barabbas is in prison with the insurrectionists who have committed murder in the uprising. The crowd comes up and asks Pilate to do for them what he usually does. Pilate asks: do you want me to release the king of the Jews? — knowing it's out of self-interest that the chief priests have handed Jesus over. But the chief priests stir up the crowd to have Pilate release Barabbas instead. Pilate asks: what shall I do with the one you call the king of the Jews? Crucify him! they shout. Why? What crime has he committed? Pilate asks. But they shout all the louder: crucify him! Wanting to satisfy the crowd, Pilate releases Barabbas to them. He has Jesus flogged and hands him over to be crucified. Barabbas — whose name means 'son of the father' — is released while the actual Son of the Father takes his place. The guilty man goes free; the innocent man dies. The substitution is the gospel in miniature, played out in a Roman courtyard.

The soldiers lead Jesus away into the palace (the Praetorium) and call together the whole company of soldiers. They put a purple robe on him, twist together a crown of thorns and set it on him, and begin to call out: hail, king of the Jews! Again and again they strike him on the head with a staff and spit on him. Falling on their knees, they pay homage to him. After they've mocked him, they take off the purple robe and put his own clothes on him. Then they lead him out to crucify him. Every element of the mockery is ironic: the purple robe, the crown, the acclamation, the kneeling. They think they're ridiculing a pretender. They're actually performing a coronation. The king they're crowning with thorns really is the king of the Jews — and the king of everything else.

A certain man from Cyrene, Simon, the father of Alexander and Rufus, is passing by on his way in from the country. They force him to carry the cross. They bring Jesus to the place called Golgotha — which means the Place of the Skull. They offer him wine mixed with myrrh, but he doesn't take it. They crucify him. Dividing up his clothes, they cast lots to see what each will get. It's nine in the morning when they crucify him. The written notice of the charge against him reads: the king of the Jews. They crucify two rebels with him, one on his right and one on his left. Those who pass by hurl insults, shaking their heads: so, you who are going to destroy the temple and build it in three days — come down from the cross and save yourself! In the same way the chief priests and the teachers of the law mock him among themselves: he saved others, but he can't save himself! Let this Messiah, this king of Israel, come down now from the cross, that we may see and believe. Those crucified with him also heap insults on him. Mark names Simon's sons — Alexander and Rufus — because his first readers apparently know them. The mockery is unknowing prophecy: he saved others precisely because he can't (won't) save himself. Coming down from the cross would destroy its purpose.

At noon, darkness comes over the whole land until three in the afternoon. At three in the afternoon Jesus cries out in a loud voice: Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani? — which means 'my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' Some standing near hear this and say: listen, he's calling Elijah. Someone runs, fills a sponge with wine vinegar, puts it on a staff, and offers it to Jesus to drink: now leave him alone. Let's see if Elijah comes to take him down. With a loud cry, Jesus breathes his last. The curtain of the temple is torn in two from top to bottom. When the centurion, who stands there in front of Jesus, sees how he dies, he says: surely this man was the Son of God! Some women are watching from a distance — among them Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the younger and of Joses, and Salome. In Galilee these women followed him and cared for his needs. Many other women who have come up with him to Jerusalem are also there. Jesus' cry is Psalm 22:1 — a psalm that begins with abandonment but ends with vindication. Mark preserves the Aramaic: you hear Jesus' actual voice. The temple curtain tears 'from top to bottom' — God tears it, from his side. The barrier between God and humanity is removed at the moment of Jesus' death. And the first person to confess Jesus as the Son of God is a Roman centurion — a Gentile executioner standing at the cross. In Mark's Gospel, no human being makes this confession until Jesus is dead. You can only understand who Jesus is by watching him die.

It's Preparation Day — the day before the Sabbath. As evening approaches, Joseph of Arimathea, a prominent member of the Council who is himself waiting for the kingdom of God, goes boldly to Pilate and asks for Jesus' body. Pilate is surprised to hear that he's already dead. Summoning the centurion, he asks if Jesus has already died. When he learns from the centurion that it is so, he gives the body to Joseph. Joseph buys some linen cloth, takes down the body, wraps it in the linen, and places it in a tomb cut out of rock. He rolls a stone against the entrance of the tomb. Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses see where he's laid. Mark confirms the death multiple ways — Pilate verifies it through the centurion. Joseph's boldness is notable: a Council member publicly claiming the body of a condemned criminal is a career-ending move. The women watch where the body is placed. They'll be the first witnesses.
